title: EXPOSÉ I ALGEBRAIC STRUCTURES. COHOMOLOGY OF GROUPS

pages.title: EXPOSÉ I ALGEBRAIC STRUCTURES. COHOMOLOGY OF GROUPS

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ I
ALGEBRAIC STRUCTURES. COHOMOLOGY OF GROUPSby M. Demazure

This exposé consists of two parts; the first collects a certain number of general definitions and fixes notation that will often be used in the sequel, while the second treats the cohomology of groups and culminates in Theorem 5.3.3 (vanishing of the cohomology of diagonalizable groups).

We choose once and for all a Universe. All the definitions given and all the constructions carried out will be relative to this Universe. We will systematically allow ourselves the following abuse of language: to define a functor \(f : \mathscr{C} \to \mathscr{C}'\), we will content ourselves with defining the object \(f(S)\) of \(\mathscr{C}'\) for every object S of \(\mathscr{C}\), whenever there is no ambiguity about how to define \(f(h)\) for an arrow \(h\) of \(\mathscr{C}\). In practice, we will say: let \(f : \mathscr{C} \to \mathscr{C}'\) be the functor defined by \(f(S) = \cdots\).
1. Generalities

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category. We denote by \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) the category \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathscr{C}^{\circ}, (\mathrm{Ens}))\) of contravariant functors from \(\mathscr{C}\) to the category \((\mathrm{Ens})\) of sets. There is a canonical functor \(h : \mathscr{C} \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) which associates with every \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\) the functor \(h_X\) such that \[ h_X(S) = \mathrm{Hom}(S, X). \]

For every functor \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}})\), one defines (cf. for example EGA 0.8.1) a bijection \[ \mathrm{Hom}(h_X, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } F(X). \] In particular, for every pair \(X, Y\) of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\), the canonical map \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X, Y) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(h_X, h_Y) \] is bijective; the functor \(h\) is fully faithful. It therefore defines an isomorphism of \(\mathscr{C}\) onto a full subcategory of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), and an equivalence of \(\mathscr{C}\) with the full subcategory of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) consisting of the representable functors (i.e. those isomorphic to a functor of the form \(h_X\)). In what follows, we will often identify X and \(h_X\). The purpose of the following numbers is to show that this identification can be made without danger.

A monomorphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) is nothing other than a morphism \(F \to G\) such that for \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), the corresponding map of sets \(F(S) \to G(S)\) is injective. We say that F is a subobject (or a subfunctor) of G if \(F(S)\) is a subset of \(G(S)\) for each S.

In \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), "arbitrary" projective limits exist and are computed by \[ (\varprojlim_i F_i)(S) = \varprojlim_i F_i(S), \] in particular fibred products by \[ (F \underset{G}{\times} F')(S) = F(S) \underset{G(S)}{\times} F'(S). \]

We choose as final object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) the functor \(\underline{e}\) such that \(\underline{e}(S) = \{\varnothing\}\). Every \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}})\) has a unique morphism to \(\underline{e}\) and we set \[ F \times F' = F \underset{\underline{e}}{\times} F'. \]

For every \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}})\) we set \[ \Gamma(F) = \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{e}, F); \] an element of \(\Gamma(F)\) is thus a family \((\gamma_S)_{S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})}\), \(\gamma_S \in F(S)\), such that for every arrow \(f : S' \to S''\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), we have \(F(f)(\gamma_{S''}) = \gamma_{S'}\).

The functor \(h\) commutes with projective limits; in particular, for \(X \times X'\) to exist (\(X, X' \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\)), resp. for \(\mathscr{C}\) to admit a final object \(e\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(h_X \times h_{X'}\) be representable, resp. that \(\underline{e}\) be representable, and we have \[ h_X \times h_{X'} \simeq h_{X \times X'} \ , \qquad h_e \simeq \underline{e}. \] We set \(\Gamma(X) = \Gamma(h_X)\) for \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\). If \(\mathscr{C}\) has a final object \(e\), we thus have an isomorphism \(\Gamma(X) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(e, X)\).

Let \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\). We denote by \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) the category of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) over S, that is, the category whose objects are the arrows \(f : T \to S\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), the set \(\mathrm{Hom}(f, f')\) being the subset of \(\mathrm{Hom}(T, T')\) consisting of the u such that \(f = f' \circ u\). If \(\mathscr{C}\) has a final object \(e\), then \(\mathscr{C}_{/e}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathscr{C}\). The category \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) has a final object: the identity arrow \(S \to S\).

If \(f : T \to S\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), then we can form the category \((\mathscr{C}_{/S})_{/f}\), which by abuse of language we denote \((\mathscr{C}_{/S})_{/T}\), and we have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathscr{C}_{/T} \simeq (\mathscr{C}_{/S})_{/T}. \] This construction applies in particular to the categories \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\), etc.; in particular one defines the category \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}_{/h_S}\).

If \(f : T \to S\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), then \(\Gamma(f)\) is identified with the set \(\Gamma(T/S)\) of sections of T over S, that is, of arrows \(S \to T\) that are right inverses of \(f\). Note that \(h_f : h_T \to h_S\) is then an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S\) and that we have: \[ \Gamma(h_f) \simeq \Gamma(h_T/h_S) \simeq \Gamma(T/S) \simeq \Gamma(f). \]

We now propose to define an equivalence between the categories \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\) and \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S\), that is, to prove that "giving a functor on the category of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) over S is 'the same thing' as giving a functor on \(\mathscr{C}\) equipped with a morphism to \(h_S\)".
(i) Construction of \(\alpha_S : \widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\).

Let first \(H : F \to h_S\) be an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S\). We must define a functor \(\alpha_S(H)\) on \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\). So let first \(f : T \to S\) be an object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\); define \(\alpha_S(H)(f)\) as the inverse image of \(f \in h_S(T)\) under the map \(H(T) : F(T) \to h_S(T)\).

Next let \(u : f \to f'\) be an arrow of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\); then \(F(u) : F(T') \to F(T)\) induces a map from \(\alpha_S(H)(f')\) to \(\alpha_S(H)(f)\), which we denote \(\alpha_S(H)(u)\). One checks at once that the maps \[ f \longmapsto \alpha_S(H)(f) \ , \qquad u \longmapsto \alpha_S(H)(u) \] indeed define a functor on \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), hence an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\).

Finally, let \(H : F \to h_S\) and \(H' : F' \to h_S\) be two objects of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S\) and \(U : H \to H'\) a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S\), \[ \xymatrix{ F \ar[rr]^-{U} \ar[dr]_-{H} & & F' \ar[dl]^-{H'} \\ & h_S & } \]

Then for every \(f : T \to S\), \(U(T) : F(T) \to F'(T)\) induces a map \[ \alpha_S(U)(f) : \alpha_S(H)(f) \longrightarrow \alpha_S(H')(f), \] which defines a morphism of functors \[ \alpha_S(U) : \alpha_S(H) \longrightarrow \alpha_S(H'). \] One easily checks that the maps \[ H \longmapsto \alpha_S(H) \ , \qquad U \longmapsto \alpha_S(U) \] indeed define a functor \(\alpha_S : \widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4.1

The functor \(\alpha_S : \widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\) is an equivalence of categories.

Let us indicate only the principle of the construction of a quasi-inverse functor \(\beta_S : \widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}} \to \widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S\). Let G be a functor on \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\); for every object T of \(\mathscr{C}\), we set \[ \beta_S(G)(T) = \text{sum of the sets } G(f) \text{ for } f \in \mathrm{Hom}(T, S) = h_S(T), \] which defines a functor \(\beta_S(G)\) on \(\mathscr{C}\), equipped with an obvious projection onto \(h_S\).

The equivalence \(\alpha_S\) commutes with the functors \(\Gamma\). In other words, if \(H : F \to h_S\) is an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S\) and \(\alpha_S(H)\) the corresponding object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\), we have \[ \Gamma(\alpha_S(H)) \simeq \Gamma(H) \simeq \Gamma(F/h_S). \] The equivalence \(\alpha_S\) commutes with the functors \(h\): if \(f : T \to S\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), \(h_f : h_T \to h_S\) is an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S\) whose transform under \(\alpha_S\) is none other than \(h_{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}(f)\), where \[ h_{\mathscr{C}_{/S}} : \mathscr{C}_{/S} \longrightarrow \widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}} \] is the canonical functor. Consequently:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.5.1

Let \(H : F \to h_S\) be an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S\). For \(\alpha_S(H) : \mathscr{C}_{/S} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) to be representable, it is necessary and sufficient that \(F : \mathscr{C} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) be representable; if \(F \simeq h_T\), then \(\alpha_S(H)\) is representable by the object \(T \to S\) of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\).

The equivalence \(\alpha_S\) is transitive in S: if \(T \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C}_{/S})\), we have a commutative diagram of equivalences \[ \xymatrix{ (\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S)_{/h_T} \ar[rr]^-{\alpha_S/h_T} \ar[d]_-{\simeq} & & (\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}})_{/h_T} \ar[r]^-{\alpha_{h_T}} & (\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}})_{/T} \ar[d]^-{\simeq} \\ \widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_T \ar[rrr]_-{\alpha_T} & & & \widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/T}} } \] where \(\alpha_S/h_T\) denotes (provisionally) the restriction (cf. 1.6) of the functor \(\alpha_S\) to the objects over \(h_T\).
1.6. Base change in a functor.

For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), we have a canonical functor \[ i_S : \mathscr{C}_{/S} \longrightarrow \mathscr{C} \] defined by \(i_S(f) = T\) if \(f\) is the arrow \(T \to S\). If \(f : T \to S\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), we write \(i_{T/S} = i_f\): \[ i_{T/S} : (\mathscr{C}_{/S})_{/T} \longrightarrow \mathscr{C}_{/S}, \] and we have the commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ (\mathscr{C}_{/S})_{/T} \ar[r]^-{i_{T/S}} \ar[d]_-{\simeq} & \mathscr{C}_{/S} \ar[d]^-{i_S} \\ \mathscr{C}_{/T} \ar[r]_-{i_T} & \mathscr{C} } \] that is, identifying \((\mathscr{C}_{/S})_{/T}\) with \(\mathscr{C}_{/T}\) as we will do from now on, \[ i_S \circ i_{T/S} = i_T. \] In the same way, if we identify \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(\mathscr{C}_{/e}\) when \(\mathscr{C}\) has a final object \(e\), then \(i_{S/e} : \mathscr{C}_{/S} \to \mathscr{C}_{/e}\) is identified with \(i_S\).

For \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\) (resp. \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C}_{/S})\)), let \(p_S(X)\) (resp. \(p_{T/S}(X)\)) be the object of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) (resp. of \(\mathscr{C}_{/T}\)), when it exists, defined by \(X \times S\) (resp. \(X \times_S T\)) equipped with its second projection: \[ \xymatrix{ X\times S \ar[d]^-{p_S(X)} \\ S } \qquad \text{resp.} \qquad \xymatrix{ X\times_S T \ar[d]^-{p_{T/S}(X)} \\ T } \]

The (partially defined) functor \(p_S\) (resp. \(p_{T/S}\)) is called the base change functor. By definition of the product (resp. of the fibred product), it is the adjoint functor of the functor \(i_S\) (resp. \(i_{T/S}\)). We also write \[ p_S(X) = X_S \ , \qquad p_{T/S}(X) = X_T. \] The functor \(i_S\) defines a functor (restriction) \[ i_S^* : \widehat{\mathscr{C}} \longrightarrow \widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}; \] where we write \(F_S = i_S^*(F) = F \circ i_S^{\circ}\). We obviously have \[ i_{T/S}^* \circ i_S^* = i_T^*, \] that is, for every functor \(F \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}})\), \[ (F_S)_T = F_T. \] The notation calls for a justification, which is as follows:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.6.1

For the functor \((h_X)_S : (\mathscr{C}_{/S})^{\circ} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) to be representable, it is necessary and sufficient that the product \(X \times S\) exist. We then have \[ (h_X)_S \simeq h_{X_S}. \]

This shows that \(F_S\) has two interpretations: the restriction of the functor F to \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), and the functor obtained by the base change \(e \leftarrow S\). This leads to the following notation: \[ \xymatrix{ F \ar@{-}[d] & F_S \ar@{-}[d] & F_T \ar@{-}[d] \\ e & S \ar[l] & T \ar[l] } \] which accounts well for the two preceding interpretations. Note that we have \[ \Gamma(F_S) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(h_S, F) \simeq F(S), \] in particular \[ \Gamma(X_S) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(S, X). \]
1.7. Objects Hom, Isom, ...

Let F and G be two objects of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). We are going to define another object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) in the following way: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}}(F_S, G_S) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}/h_S}(F\times h_S, G\times h_S) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}}(F\times h_S, G). \] The object \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)\) defined above has the following properties:

1. (i)

    \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\underline{e}, G) \simeq G\)

  2. (ii)

    The formation of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}\) commutes with base extension: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F_S, G_S) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)_S. \]

  3. (iii)

    \((F, G) \mapsto \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)\) is a bifunctor, contravariant in F and covariant in G.

These three properties are obvious from the definitions. Let E be a third object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). We are going to define a morphism of trifunctors \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E \times F, G) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)). \]

Let \(f : E \times F \to G\); we must associate with it a morphism from E to \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)\). So let \(S' \to S\) be an arrow of \(\mathscr{C}\). We have maps \[ E(S) \times F(S') \longrightarrow E(S') \times F(S') \xrightarrow{\ f(S')\ } G(S'). \] An element of \(E(S)\) therefore defines, for every \(S' \to S\), a map \(F(S') \to G(S')\), functorial in \(S'\), hence an element of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)(S)\). We leave it to the reader to check that we have indeed constructed a morphism of trifunctors in this way.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.7.1

The morphism of functors \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E \times F, G) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)) \] is an isomorphism.

Let us indicate only a principle of proof. Consider both sides as functors in E. The asserted result is true if \(E = h_X\); indeed, in that case it is nothing other than the definition of the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)\). On the other hand, both sides, as functors in E, transform inductive limits into projective limits. Finally, every object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) is an inductive limit of objects of the form \(h_X\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

We have \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(E,G))\). In particular, taking \(E = \underline{e}\), and taking into account \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\underline{e},G) \simeq G\), we have \[ \Gamma(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(F,G). \] Note that composition of the \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}\) yields functorial morphisms \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G) \times \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,H) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,H). \] If F and G are two objects of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), we denote by \(\mathrm{Isom}(F,G)\) the subset of \(\mathrm{Hom}(F,G)\) consisting of the isomorphisms from F onto G. One then defines a subobject \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(F,G)\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G)\) by: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(F,G)(S) = \mathrm{Isom}(F_S, G_S). \]

We then have isomorphisms \[ \Gamma(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(F,G)) \simeq \mathrm{Isom}(F,G), \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(F,G) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(G,F). \] In the particular case where \(F = G\), we set \[ \underline{\mathrm{End}}(F) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,F) \ , \qquad \mathrm{End}(F) = \mathrm{Hom}(F,F) \simeq \Gamma(\underline{\mathrm{End}}(F)), \] \[ \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(F) = \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(F,F) \ , \qquad \mathrm{Aut}(F) = \mathrm{Isom}(F,F) \simeq \Gamma(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(F)). \] The formation of the objects \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}\), \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}\) commutes with base changes. Note that one can construct an object isomorphic to \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(F,G)\) in the following way: we have a morphism \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G) \times \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,F) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{End}}(F); \] permuting F and G, we deduce a morphism \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G) \times \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,F) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{End}}(F) \times \underline{\mathrm{End}}(G). \] On the other hand, the identity morphism of F is an element of \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}(F)\) and therefore defines a morphism \(\underline{e} \to \underline{\mathrm{End}}(F)\). Doing the same with G and taking the product, we find a morphism \[ \underline{e} \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{End}}(F) \times \underline{\mathrm{End}}(G). \] It is then immediate that the fibred product of \(\underline{e}\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F,G) \times \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,F)\) over \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}(F) \times \underline{\mathrm{End}}(G)\) is isomorphic to \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(F,G)\). All these definitions apply in particular to the case where \(F = h_X\), \(G = h_Y\). In the case where \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(h_X, h_Y)\) is representable by an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), we denote this object by \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(X, Y)\). It has the following property: if \(Z \times X\) exists, then \[ \mathrm{Hom}(Z, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(X,Y)) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(Z \times X, Y). \] This property characterizes it when products exist in \(\mathscr{C}\). One defines in the same way (when they are willing to exist) objects \[ \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(X,Y) \ , \qquad \underline{\mathrm{End}}(X) \ , \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(X); \] let us simply note that, by the construction given above, \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}(X,Y)\) exists whenever fibred products exist in \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(X,Y)\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(Y,X)\), \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}(X)\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}(Y)\) exist. This also applies to categories of the form \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\). The corresponding objects will be denoted as explicitly as possible by appropriate symbols: for example, if T and T' are two objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) over S, we will write \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(T,T')\) for the object \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}(T/S, T'/S)\), which will thus be an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) over S, ...
1.8. Constant objects.

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category in which direct sums and fibred products exist and in which direct sums commute with base change (for example the category of schemes). For every set E and every object S of \(\mathscr{C}\), let \(E_S\) be the direct sum of a family \((S_i)_{i \in E}\) of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) all isomorphic to S. This object is characterized by the formula \[ \mathrm{Hom}(E_S, T) = \mathrm{Hom}(E, \mathrm{Hom}(S,T)), \qquad T \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C}), \] where the second Hom is taken in the category of sets. The object \(E_S\) is equipped with a canonical projection onto S, in such a way that \(E \mapsto E_S\) is in fact a functor from \((\mathrm{Ens})\) to \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\). Suppose that, \(\varnothing\) denoting an initial object of \(\mathscr{C}\), the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \varnothing \ar[r] \ar[d] & S \ar[d] \\ S \ar[r] & S \sqcup S } \] is cartesian (this is the case for the category of schemes). Then the functor \(E \mapsto E_S\) commutes with finite projective limits. In particular: \[ E_S \underset{S}{\times} F_S = (E \times F)_S. \]

If \(S' \to S\) is an arrow of \(\mathscr{C}\), we have \[ E_{S'} = (E_S)_{S'}. \] In particular, if \(\mathscr{C}\) has a final object \(e\), we have \[ E_S = (E_e)_S. \] An object of the form \(E_S\) will be called a constant object. Note that we have a monomorphism, functorial in E: \[ E \longrightarrow \Gamma(E_S/S) \] which associates with each \(i \in E\) the section of \(E_S\) over S defined by the isomorphism of S onto \(S_i\). If \(\mathscr{C}\) is the category of schemes, then \(\Gamma(E_S/S)\) is identified with the locally constant maps from the topological space S to the set E, the preceding map associating with each element of E the corresponding constant map. Note that it follows from what we have just said that \(E_S\) can also be defined as representing the functor which associates with every \(S'\) over S the set of locally constant functions from the topological space \(S'\) to the set E.
2. Algebraic structures
Given a species of algebraic structure in the category of sets, we propose to extend it to the category \(\mathscr{C}\). Let us first treat an example: the case of groups.
2.1. Group structures.
We keep the notation of the preceding section.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1.1

Let \(G \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}})\). A structure of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group on G is the datum, for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), of a group structure on the set \(G(S)\), in such a way that for every arrow \(f : S' \to S''\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), the map \(G(f) : G(S'') \to G(S')\) is a group homomorphism. If G and H are two \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups, a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups from G to H is any morphism \(u \in \mathrm{Hom}(G,H)\) such that for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\) the map of sets \(u(S) : G(S) \to H(S)\) is a group homomorphism. We denote by \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}}(G,H)\) the set of morphisms of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups from G to H, and by \((\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.})\) the category of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups.

Examples. — Let \(E \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}})\); the object \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(E)\) is equipped in an obvious way with a structure of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group. The final object \(\underline{e}\) has a unique \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group structure, which makes it a final object of \((\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.})\). For every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), let \(e_G(S)\) be the identity element of \(G(S)\). The family of the \(e_G(S)\) defines an element \(e_G \in \Gamma(G) = \mathrm{Hom}(\underline{e}, G)\), which is in fact a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \(\underline{e} \to G\) and which is called the identity section of G. Note that giving a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group structure on G amounts to giving a composition law on G, that is, a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-morphism \[ \pi_G : G \times G \longrightarrow G \] such that for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), \(\pi_G(S)\) equips \(G(S)\) with a group structure. In the same way, \(u : G \to H\) is a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups if and only if the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ G\times G \ar[r]^-{\pi_G} \ar[d]_-{(u,u)} & G \ar[d]^-{u} \\ H\times H \ar[r]_-{\pi_H} & H } \]

A subobject H of G such that, for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), \(H(S)\) is a subgroup of \(G(S)\) obviously has a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group structure induced by that of G: it is the only one for which the monomorphism \(H \to G\) is a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups. The \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group H equipped with this structure is called a sub-\(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group of G. If G and H are two \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups, the product \(G \times H\) is equipped with an obvious \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group structure: for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), we equip \(G(S) \times H(S)\) with the product group structure of the given group structures on \(G(S)\) and \(H(S)\). The \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group \(G \times H\) equipped with this structure will be called the product \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group of G and H (it is, moreover, the product in the category of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups).

If G is a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group, then for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), \(G_S\) is a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\)-group. If G and H are two \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups, one defines the object \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}}(G,H)\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) by: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}}(G,H)(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\text{-Gr.}}(G_S, H_S) \] (Nota: \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}}\) is not in general a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group, nor a fortiori the object \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}\) in the category \((\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.})\)). One defines in the same way the objects \[ \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}}(G,H) \ , \qquad \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}}(G) \ , \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}}(G). \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1.2

Let \(G \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\). A structure of \(\mathscr{C}\)-group on G is a structure of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group on \(h_G \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}})\). A morphism from the \(\mathscr{C}\)-group G to the \(\mathscr{C}\)-group H is an element \(u \in \mathrm{Hom}(G,H) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(h_G, h_H)\) which defines a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups from \(h_G\) to \(h_H\). We denote by \((\mathscr{C}\text{-Gr.})\) the category of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups. Note that there is in \((\mathrm{Cat})\) a cartesian square \[ \xymatrix{ (\mathscr{C}\text{-Gr.}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & (\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}) \ar[d] \\ \mathscr{C} \ar[r]_-{h} & \widehat{\mathscr{C}} } \]

All the preceding definitions and constructions therefore carry over immediately to \((\mathscr{C}\text{-Gr.})\) whenever the functors they involve (products, objects \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}\), ...) are representable. They also apply to the categories \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\). In that case, we will write \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-Gr.}}\) for \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\text{-Gr.}}\), etc.

More generally, if (T) is a species of structure on n base sets defined by finite projective limits (for example, by commutativities of diagrams built from cartesian products: structures of monoid, group, set with operators, module over a ring, Lie algebra over a ring, ...), the preceding construction makes it possible to define the notion of "structure of species (T) on n objects \(F_1, \ldots, F_n\) of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)": such a structure will be the datum, for each S of \(\mathscr{C}\), of a structure of species (T) on the sets \(F_1(S), \ldots, F_n(S)\) in such a way that for every arrow \(S' \to S''\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), the family of maps \((F_i(S'')) \to (F_i(S'))\) is a poly-homomorphism for the species of structure (T). One defines similarly the morphisms of the species of structure (T), whence a category \((\widehat{\mathscr{C}} \times \widehat{\mathscr{C}} \cdots \times \widehat{\mathscr{C}})^{(T)}\). The fully faithful functor \((h \times h \times \cdots \times h)\) then makes it possible to define, by inverse image, the category \((\mathscr{C} \times \mathscr{C} \cdots \times \mathscr{C})^{(T)}\), and then, since it commutes with projective limits, to transport to it all the functorial properties, notions and notations introduced in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). Suppose now that fibred products exist in \(\mathscr{C}\), and let (T) be a species of algebraic structure defined by the datum of certain morphisms between cartesian products satisfying axioms consisting of certain commutativities of diagrams built from the preceding arrows. A structure of species (T) on a family of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) will therefore be defined by certain morphisms between cartesian products satisfying certain commutation conditions. It follows that if \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(\mathscr{C}'\) are two categories having products and \(f : \mathscr{C} \to \mathscr{C}'\) is a functor commuting with products, then for every family of objects \((F_i)\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) equipped with a structure of species (T), the family \((f(F_i))\) of objects of \(\mathscr{C}'\) will thereby itself be equipped with a structure of species (T). Every \(\mathscr{C}\)-group will be transformed into a \(\mathscr{C}'\)-group, every pair (\(\mathscr{C}\)-ring, \(\mathscr{C}\)-module over that \(\mathscr{C}\)-ring) into an analogous pair in \(\mathscr{C}'\)... Let in particular \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category satisfying the conditions stated in 1.8; the functor \(E \mapsto E_S\) defined loc. cit. commutes with projective limits; it therefore transforms groups into S-groups (i.e. \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\) groups), rings into S-rings...

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

It is worth noting that the preceding construction process, applied to the category \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), does recover the notions already defined there; in other words, it amounts to the same thing to give, on an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), a structure of species (T) when this object is considered as a functor on \(\mathscr{C}\), or to give a structure of species (T) on the representable functor on \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) defined by this object.
We will treat two more particular cases of the preceding construction: the case of structures with a group of operators and the case of modules.
2.3. Structures with groups of operators.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.1

Let \(E \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}})\) and \(G \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.})\). A structure of object with \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group of operators G (or of G-object) on E is the datum on \(E(S)\), for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), of a structure of set with group of operators \(G(S)\), in such a way that, for every arrow \(S' \to S''\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), the map of sets \(E(S'') \to E(S')\) is compatible with the homomorphism of operators \(G(S'') \to G(S')\). As usual, it amounts to the same thing to give a morphism \[ \mu : G \times E \longrightarrow E \] which for every S equips \(E(S)\) with a structure of set with operators \(G(S)\). But \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G \times E, E) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G, \underline{\mathrm{End}}(E))\), so \(\mu\) defines a morphism \(G \to \underline{\mathrm{End}}(E)\), and it is immediate that the latter maps G into \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(E)\) and that it is a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups. Consequently: giving on E a structure of object with \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group of operators G is equivalent to giving a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \[ \rho : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(E). \]

In particular, every element \(g \in G(S)\) defines an automorphism \(\rho(g)\) of the functor \(E_S\), that is, an automorphism of \(E \times h_S\) commuting with the projection \(E \times h_S \to h_S\), and in particular an automorphism of the set \(E(S')\) for every \(S' \to S\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.2

We denote by \(E^G\) the subobject of E defined as follows: \[ E^G(S) = \{x \in E(S) \mid x_{S'} \text{ invariant under } G(S') \text{ for every } S' \to S\}, \] where \(x_{S'}\) denotes the image of \(x\) under \(E(S) \to E(S')\). Then \(E^G\) ("subobject of invariants of G") is the largest subobject of E on which G operates trivially.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.3

Let F be a subobject of E. We denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G F\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G F\) the sub-\(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups of G defined by \[ (\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G F)(S) = \{g \in G(S) \mid \rho(g)F_S \subset F_S\} = \{g \in G(S) \mid \rho(g)F(S') \subset F(S'), \text{ for every } S' \to S\}, \] \[ (\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G F)(S) = \{g \in G(S) \mid \rho(g)|_{F_S} = \text{identity}\} = \{g \in G(S) \mid \rho(g)|_{F(S')} = \text{identity}, \text{ for every } S' \to S\}, \] where the vertical bar after \(\rho(g)\) denotes restriction.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.4

If G is a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group and E an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) (resp. E an object of \(\mathscr{C}\)), a structure of G-object on E (resp. on E) is a structure of \(h_G\)-object on E (resp. \(h_E\)). In view of this definition, all the notions and notations defined above carry over to \(\mathscr{C}\), when they involve only representable functors: for example, if \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{h_G}(E)\) is representable, then there is one and only one subobject of G which represents it, and which is then a sub-\(\mathscr{C}\)-group of G; we denote it by \(\mathrm{Norm}_G(E)\) ...

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.5

a) We say that the \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group G operates on the \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group H if H is equipped with a G-group structure such that, for every \(g \in G(S)\), the automorphism of \(H(S)\) defined by g is a group automorphism. It amounts to the same thing to say that for every \(g \in G(S)\), the automorphism \(\rho(g)\) of \(H_S\) is an automorphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}_{/S}}\)-groups, or again that the morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \(G \to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(H)\) maps G into \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}}(H)\).

b) In the above situation, there is on the product \(H \times G\) a unique \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group structure such that, for every S, \(H(S) \times G(S)\) is the semi-direct product of the groups \(H(S)\) and \(G(S)\) relative to the given operation of \(G(S)\) on \(H(S)\). We will denote this \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group by \(H \cdot G\) and call it the semi-direct product of H by G. By definition we thus have \[ (H \cdot G)(S) = H(S) \cdot G(S). \] Let G be a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group. For every arrow \(S' \to S\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and every \(g \in G(S)\), let \(\mathrm{Int}(g)\) be the automorphism of \(G(S')\) defined by \(\mathrm{Int}(g)h = ghg^{-1}\). This definition extends to that of a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \[ \mathrm{Int} : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\text{-Gr.}}(G) \subset \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(G). \] Definition 2.3.3 therefore applies, and we have sub-\(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups of G \[ \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(E) \qquad \text{and} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(E) \] for every subobject E of G.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.6

We call the centre of G, and denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(G)\), the sub-\(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(G)\) of G. We say that G is commutative if \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(G) = G\) or, what amounts to the same thing, if \(G(S)\) is commutative for every S. We say that the sub-\(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group H of G is normal in G if \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H) = G\), or, what amounts to the same thing, if \(H(S)\) is normal in \(G(S)\) for every S. Many definitions and propositions of elementary group theory carry over easily; let us simply point out the following one, which will be useful to us:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3.7

Let \(f : W \to G\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups. Set \(H(S) = \mathrm{Ker}\, f(S)\). Let \(u : G \to W\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups which is a section of \(f\) (and which is then necessarily a monomorphism). Then W is identified with the semi-direct product of H by G for the operation of G on H defined by \((g,h) \mapsto \mathrm{Int}(u(g))h\) for \(g \in G(S)\), \(h \in H(S)\), \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\).

All these definitions and propositions carry over as usual to \(\mathscr{C}\). In particular, one defines the semi-direct product of two \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups H and G (G operating on H) when the cartesian product \(H \times G\) exists, and we have the analogue of Proposition 2.3.7 in the following form:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3.8

Let \(H \xrightarrow{\ i\ } W \xrightarrow{\ f\ } G\) be a sequence of morphisms of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups such that, for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), \((H(S), i(S))\) is a kernel of \(f(S) : W(S) \to G(S)\). Let \(u : G \to W\) be a morphism of \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups which is a section of \(f\). Then W is identified with the semi-direct product of H by G for the operation of G on H defined by: for \(g \in G(S)\) and \(h \in H(S)\), we have \[ \mathrm{Int}(u(g))\, i(h) = i({}^{g}h). \]

pages.title: 3. The category of O-modules, the category of G-O-modules

blocks.html: 3. The category of O-modules, the category of G-O-modules

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

Let O and F be two objects of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). We say that F is a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-module over the \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-ring O, or for short an O-module, if, for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), we have equipped \(O(S)\) with a ring structure and \(F(S)\) with a module structure over this ring, in such a way that for every arrow \(S' \to S''\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(O(S'') \to O(S')\) is a ring homomorphism and \(F(S'') \to F(S')\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups compatible with this ring homomorphism. If O is fixed, one defines in the usual way a morphism of the O-modules F and F', whence the commutative group \(\mathrm{Hom}_O(F,F')\), and the category of O-modules, denoted \((O\text{-Mod.})\). Note that if \(O_0\) is the \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-ring defined by \(O_0(S) = \mathbb{Z}\) (which must not be confused with the functor associated with the constant object \(\mathbb{Z}\)), then the category of \(O_0\)-modules is isomorphic to the category of commutative \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups. The category \((O\text{-Mod.})\) is abelian. Note that, if F is an O-module, then for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), \(F_S\) is an \(O_S\)-module. One can therefore define an abelian \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_O(F,F')\) by \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_O(F,F')(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_{O_S}(F_S, F'_S). \] One defines in the same way objects \[ \underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_O(F,F') \ , \qquad \underline{\mathrm{End}}_O(F) \ , \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_O(F), \] the last being a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group for the structure induced by composition of automorphisms.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.2

Let O be a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-ring, F an O-module and G a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group. A structure of G-O-module on F is a structure of G-object such that for \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\) and every \(g \in G(S)\), the automorphism of \(F(S)\) defined by g is an automorphism of its \(O(S)\)-module structure. It amounts to the same thing to say that the morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \[ \rho : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(F) \] defined in 2.3 maps G into the sub-\(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_O(F)\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}(F)\). To give a structure of G-O-module on the O-module F is thus to give a morphism of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups \[ \rho : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_O(F). \] One defines in a natural way the abelian group \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{G\text{-}O}(F,F'), \] hence the additive category of G-O-modules, denoted \((G\text{-}O\text{-Mod.})\). The reader will note that the latter can also be defined as the category of \(O[G]\)-modules, where \(O[G]\) is the algebra of the \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group G over the \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-ring O, whose definition is clear.

All the constructions of this section specialize at once to the case where G (or O, or both) are representable by objects of \(\mathscr{C}\), which are thereby equipped with the corresponding algebraic structures. We have treated succinctly the case of the main algebraic structures encountered in the sequel of this seminar. For the others (the structure of O-Lie algebra, for example), we believe that the reader will have had enough examples in this section to be able, in each particular case, to operate for himself the general mechanism sketched in 2.2. We are now going to apply what we have just done to the category of schemes, denoted (Sch), and more generally to the categories \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\).

pages.title: 4. Algebraic structures in the category of schemes

blocks.html: 4. Algebraic structures in the category of schemes

We will allow ourselves, whenever there is no ambiguity, the following abuses of language: we will denote by T the object \(T \xrightarrow{f} S\) of \(\mathscr{C}_{/S}\), the datum of \(f\) (the "structural morphism of T") being understood, and we will identify \(\mathscr{C}\) with a subcategory of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). In view of the compatibilities stated in the preceding sections, these identifications can be made without danger. We will moreover simplify the terminology on the following model: a (Sch)-group will also be called a group scheme, a \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\)-group a group scheme over S, or an S-group scheme, or an S-group, or an A-group when S is the spectrum of the ring A.
4.1. Constant schemes.

The category of schemes satisfies the conditions required in 1.8. One can therefore define constant objects in it; for every set E, we have a scheme \(E_{\mathbb{Z}}\) and, for every scheme S, an S-scheme \(E_S = (E_{\mathbb{Z}})_S\). Recall that for every scheme T, \(\mathrm{Hom}(T, E_S)\) is the set of locally constant maps from the topological space T to E. The functor \[ E \longmapsto E_S \] commutes with finite projective limits. In particular, if G is a group, \(G_S\) is an S-group scheme; if O is a ring, \(O_S\) is an S-ring scheme.
4.2. Affine S-groups.

Let us recall a certain number of facts about S-schemas affine over S (EGA II, §1). We say that the S-scheme T is affine over S if the inverse image of every affine open subset of S is affine. The \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebra \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_T)\), which we denote by \(\mathscr{A}(T)\), is then quasi-coherent (\(f\) denotes the structural morphism of T). Conversely, with every quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) one can associate an S-scheme affine over S, denoted \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}\). These functors \(T \mapsto \mathscr{A}(T)\) and \(\mathscr{A} \mapsto \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}\) are equivalences quasi-inverse to each other between the category of S-schemes affine over S and the category opposite to that of quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebras. It follows that giving an algebraic structure on an S-scheme T affine over S is equivalent to giving the corresponding structure on \(\mathscr{A}(T)\) in the category opposite to that of quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebras. In particular, if G is an S-group affine over S, \(\mathscr{A}(G)\) is equipped with a structure of augmented \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-bigebra, that is, we have two morphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebras \[ \Delta : \mathscr{A}(G) \longrightarrow \mathscr{A}(G) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{A}(G) \ , \qquad \varepsilon : \mathscr{A}(G) \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_S, \] corresponding to the morphisms of S-schemes \[ \pi : G \times G \longrightarrow G \ , \qquad e_G : S \longrightarrow G. \] The maps \(\Delta\) and \(\varepsilon\) satisfy the following conditions (which express that G is an S-monoid):

(HA 1) \(\Delta\) is co-associative: the following diagram is commutative \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathscr{A}(G) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{A}(G) \ar[dr]^-{\mathrm{Id}\otimes\Delta} & \\ \mathscr{A}(G) \ar[ur]^-{\Delta} \ar[dr]_-{\Delta} & & \mathscr{A}(G)\otimes\mathscr{A}(G)\otimes\mathscr{A}(G) \\ & \mathscr{A}(G) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{A}(G) \ar[ur]_-{\Delta\otimes\mathrm{Id}} & } \]

(HA 2): \(\Delta\) is compatible with \(\varepsilon\): the two following composites are the identity \[ \mathscr{A}(G) \xrightarrow{\ \Delta\ } \mathscr{A}(G)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}(G) \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{Id}\otimes\varepsilon\ } \mathscr{A}(G)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{O}_S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathscr{A}(G) \ , \] \[ \mathscr{A}(G) \xrightarrow{\ \Delta\ } \mathscr{A}(G)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}(G) \xrightarrow{\ \varepsilon\otimes\mathrm{Id}\ } \mathscr{O}_S\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}(G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathscr{A}(G). \] Let us take advantage of the occasion to note once more that it follows from the definition of an S-group structure that, in order to give such a structure on an S-scheme G affine over S, it is not necessary to verify anything whatsoever on \(\mathscr{A}(G)\), but simply to equip each \(G(S')\), for \(S'\) over S, with a group structure functorial in \(S'\). This remark applies mutatis mutandis to morphisms.
4.3. The groups \(\mathbb{G}_a\) and \(\mathbb{G}_m\). The ring \(\mathbb{O}\)

Let \(\mathbb{G}_a\) be the functor from \((\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ}\) to \((\mathrm{Ens})\) defined by \[ \mathbb{G}_a(S) = \Gamma(S, \mathscr{O}_S) \] equipped with the (Sch)-group structure defined by the additive group structure of the ring \(\Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\). It is representable by an affine scheme which we will denote \(\mathbb{G}_a\), and which is therefore a group scheme \[ \mathbb{G}_a = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathbb{Z}[T]. \] Indeed, \(\mathbb{G}_a(S) = \mathrm{Hom}(S,\mathbb{G}_a) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Alg.}}(\mathbb{Z}[T], \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)) \simeq \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\). For every scheme S, we therefore have an S-group affine over S, \(\mathbb{G}_{a,S}\), which corresponds to the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-bigebra \(\mathscr{O}_S[T]\), with the diagonal map and the augmentation defined by: \[ \Delta(T) = T\otimes 1 + 1\otimes T \ , \qquad \varepsilon(T) = 0. \]

Let \(\mathbb{G}_m\) be the functor from \((\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ}\) to \((\mathrm{Ens})\) defined by \[ \mathbb{G}_m(S) = \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^{\times}, \] where \(\Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^{\times}\) denotes the multiplicative group of invertible elements of the ring \(\Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)\), equipped with its natural (Sch)-group structure. It is representable by an affine scheme denoted \(\mathbb{G}_m\) \[ \mathbb{G}_m = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathbb{Z}[T, T^{-1}] = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathbb{Z}[\mathbb{Z}], \] where \(\mathbb{Z}[\mathbb{Z}]\) denotes the algebra of the group \(\mathbb{Z}\) over the ring \(\mathbb{Z}\). Indeed, \[ \mathbb{G}_m(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Alg.}}(\mathbb{Z}[T,T^{-1}], \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)) \simeq \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^{\times}. \] For every scheme S, we therefore have an S-group affine over S, denoted \(\mathbb{G}_{m,S}\), which corresponds to the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebra \(\mathscr{O}_S[\mathbb{Z}]\), with the diagonal map and the augmentation defined by: \[ \Delta(x) = x\otimes x \ , \qquad \varepsilon(x) = 1, \qquad x \in \mathbb{Z}. \]

Let \(\mathbb{O}\) be the functor \(\mathbb{G}_a\) equipped with its structure of (Sch)-ring. It is represented by the scheme \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathbb{Z}[T]\), which we will denote \(\mathbb{O}\) when we consider it as equipped with its structure of ring scheme.
For every scheme S, we therefore have an S-ring affine over S, denoted \(\mathbb{O}_S\). (Nota: in EGA II 1.7.13, \(\mathbb{O}_S\) is denoted \(S[T]\)).
4.4. Diagonalizable groups

The construction of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) generalizes as follows: let M be a commutative group and \(M_S\) the S-group scheme associated with it by the procedure of 4.1. Consider the functor defined by \[ \mathbf{D}(M)(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{groups}}(M, \mathbb{G}_m(S)) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-Gr.}}(M_S, \mathbb{G}_{m,S}). \] It is a commutative \((\widehat{\mathrm{Sch}})\)-group which is representable by a group scheme which we will denote \(\mathbf{D}(M)\); by definition we will thus have: \[ \mathbf{D}(M) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{(\mathrm{Sch})\text{-Gr.}}(M_{\mathbb{Z}}, \mathbb{G}_m). \]

Indeed, set \[ \mathbf{D}(M) = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathbb{Z}[M], \] where \(\mathbb{Z}[M]\) is the algebra of the group M over the ring \(\mathbb{Z}\); we have \[ \mathbf{D}(M)(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Alg.}}(\mathbb{Z}[M], \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{groups}}(M, \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^{\times}) \] by the very definition of the algebra \(\mathbb{Z}[M]\).

For every scheme S we therefore have an S-group scheme affine over S \[ \mathbf{D}_S(M) = \mathbf{D}(M)_S = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{(\mathrm{Sch})\text{-Gr.}}(M_{\mathbb{Z}}, \mathbb{G}_m)_S = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-Gr.}}(M_S, \mathbb{G}_{m,S}). \] It is associated with the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-bigebra \(\mathscr{O}_S[M]\), equipped with the diagonal map and the augmentation defined by \[ \Delta(x) = x\otimes x \quad \text{and} \quad \varepsilon(x) = 1, \qquad x \in M. \]

If \(f : M \to N\) is a homomorphism of commutative groups, one defines in an obvious way a morphism of S-groups \[ \mathbf{D}_S(f) : \mathbf{D}_S(N) \longrightarrow \mathbf{D}_S(M), \] whence a functor \[ \mathbf{D}_S : M \longmapsto \mathbf{D}_S(M) \] from the category of abelian groups to the category of S-groups affine over S, which can also be defined as the composite of the functor \(M \mapsto M_S\) and the functor \(M_S \mapsto \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-Gr.}}(M_S, \mathbb{G}_{m,S})\). This functor commutes with base extensions. An S-group isomorphic to a group of the form \(\mathbf{D}_S(M)\) is said to be diagonalizable. Note that the elements of M can be interpreted as certain characters of \(\mathbf{D}_S(M)\), that is, certain elements of \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-Gr.}}(\mathbf{D}_S(M), \mathbb{G}_{m,S})\). (Confer VIII 1).

Let us give some examples of diagonalizable groups. We have first \[ \mathbf{D}(\mathbb{Z}) = \mathbb{G}_m \ , \qquad \mathbf{D}(\mathbb{Z}^r) = (\mathbb{G}_m)^r. \] We set \[ \boldsymbol{\mu}_n = \mathbf{D}(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}), \] and call it the group of n-th roots of unity. Indeed, we have \[ \boldsymbol{\mu}_n(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{groups}}(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}, \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)^{\times}) = \{f \in \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S), f^n = 1\}. \] The S-group \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n,S}\) corresponds to the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebra \(\mathscr{O}_S[T]/(T^n - 1)\). Suppose in particular that S is the spectrum of a field k of characteristic \(p = n\). Setting \(T - 1 = s\), we find \[ k[T]/(T^p - 1) = k[s]/(s^p), \] which shows that the topological space underlying \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{p,k}\) is reduced to a point, the local ring of this point being the artinian k-algebra \(k[s]/(s^p)\). (In the same vein, let us point out that the S-schemes \(\mathbb{G}_{a,S}\), \(\mathbb{G}_{m,S}\), \(\mathbb{O}_S\) are smooth over S, that \(\mathbf{D}_S(M)\) is flat over S, and that it is formally smooth (resp. smooth) over S if and only if no residue characteristic of S divides the torsion of M (resp. and if moreover M is of finite type), cf. Exp. VIII, 2.1).
4.5. Other examples of groups.

The preceding procedure applies to the "classical groups" (\(\mathrm{GL}_n, \mathrm{Sp}_n, \mathrm{O}_n\dots\)). For example, one will define \(\mathbb{GL}_n\) as representing the functor \(\mathbf{GL}_n\) such that: \[ \mathbf{GL}_n(S) = \mathrm{GL}(n, \Gamma(S,\mathscr{O}_S)) = \mathrm{Aut}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{O}_S^n). \] One can construct it, for example, as the open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathbb{Z}[T_{ij}]\ 0 \leqslant i,j \leqslant n\) defined by the function \(f = \det((T_{ij}))\), that is, \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathbb{Z}[T_{ij}, f^{-1}]\).
4.6. Module functors in the category of schemes.

We propose to associate with every \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module on the scheme S an \(\mathbf{O}_S\)-module (where \(\mathbf{O}_S\) denotes the ring functor introduced in 4.3.3). This can be done in two different ways. Precisely:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.6.1

Let S be a scheme. For every \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module \(\mathscr{F}\), we denote by \(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F})\) and \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) the functors on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\) defined by: \[ \mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F})(S') = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S'}}(\mathscr{F} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{O}_{S'}, \mathscr{O}_{S'}) \ , \] \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})(S') = \Gamma(S', \mathscr{F} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{O}_{S'}) \] (where \(\mathscr{F} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{O}_{S'}\) denotes the inverse image on \(S'\) of the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module \(\mathscr{F}\)). Then \(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F})\) and \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) are equipped in an obvious way with a structure of \(\mathbf{O}_S\)-modules (recall that \(\mathbf{O}_S(S') = \Gamma(S',\mathscr{O}_{S'}) = \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{O}_S)(S')\)), in such a way that we have in fact defined two functors \(\mathbf{V}\) and \(\mathbf{W}\) from the category of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules to the category of \(\mathbf{O}_S\)-modules, \(\mathbf{V}\) being contravariant and \(\mathbf{W}\) covariant.

In the rest of this section we restrict ourselves to the case where the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules in question are quasi-coherent, that is, we consider \(\mathbf{V}\) and \(\mathbf{W}\) as functors from the category \((\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.q.c.})\) of quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules to the category of \(\mathbf{O}_S\)-modules \[ \mathbf{V} : (\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.q.c.})^{\circ} \longrightarrow (\mathbf{O}_S\text{-Mod.}), \qquad \mathbf{W} : (\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.q.c.}) \longrightarrow (\mathbf{O}_S\text{-Mod.}). \] (Nota: the reader will note that, in the sequel, all the propositions which involve only the functor \(\mathbf{W}\) are valid without this restriction).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6.2
  1. (i)

    The functors \(\mathbf{V}\) and \(\mathbf{W}\) commute with base extension: if \(S'\) is over S and \(\mathscr{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module, we have \[ \mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F} \otimes \mathscr{O}_{S'}) \simeq \mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F})_{S'} \ , \qquad \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F} \otimes \mathscr{O}_{S'}) \simeq \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})_{S'}. \]

  2. (ii)

    The functors \(\mathbf{V}\) and \(\mathbf{W}\) are fully faithful: the canonical maps \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{F}') \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}'),\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F})) \] \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{F}') \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}')) \] are bijective.

  3. (iii)

    The functors \(\mathbf{V}\) and \(\mathbf{W}\) are additive: \[ \mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}\oplus\mathscr{F}') \simeq \mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}) \underset{S}{\times} \mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}') \ , \qquad \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}\oplus\mathscr{F}') \simeq \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}) \underset{S}{\times} \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}'). \]

Parts (i) and (iii) are obvious from the definitions. For (ii), one takes for \(S'\) open subsets of S. We leave the proof to the reader (for \(\mathbf{V}\), use EGA II, 1.7.14).

We have canonical morphisms in \((\mathbf{O}_S\text{-Mod.})\): \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{F}')) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}')), \] \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{F}')) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}'),\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F})). \] This follows immediately from (i) and (ii). Let us now consider \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) and \(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F})\) as functors over S. It is known (EGA II, 1.7.8) that \(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F})\) is representable by an S-scheme affine over S, which is denoted \(\mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F})\) and called the vector bundle defined by \(\mathscr{F}\), \[ \mathbb{V}(\mathscr{F}) = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F}), \] where \(\mathscr{S}(\mathscr{F})\) denotes the symmetric algebra of the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module \(\mathscr{F}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6.4

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{F}'\) be two quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules, \(\mathscr{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebra. We have a functorial isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_S(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}'))) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{A} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{F}'). \]

Indeed, if we write \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}\), the left-hand side is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S'}}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})_{S'}, \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}')_{S'})\), that is, by definition, to \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S'}}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}), \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}'\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'})) \] (cf. 4.6.2 (i)), which by 4.6.2 (ii) can also be written \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S'}}(\mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}, \mathscr{F}'\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F}, f_*(f^*(\mathscr{F}'))), \] where \(f : S' \to S\) denotes the structural morphism. But, by EGA II, 1.4.7, we have \(f_*(f^*(\mathscr{F}')) \simeq \mathscr{F}'\otimes\mathscr{A}\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

We have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{A}\otimes\mathscr{F}) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}, \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})). \] Indeed, one takes \(\mathscr{F} = \mathscr{O}_S\) in the preceding formula; then one lets S vary.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6.5

If \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{F}'\) are two locally free \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules of finite type, the morphisms of 4.6.3 are isomorphisms.

Indeed, for every \(S' \to S\), we have \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{F}'))(S') = \Gamma(S', \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{F}')\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{S'}}(\mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}, \mathscr{F}'\otimes\mathscr{O}_{S'}). \] But the right-hand side is indeed isomorphic to \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}'))(S')\) and to \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}'),\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}))(S')\), by 4.6.2 (i) and (ii).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6.5.1

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module of finite type. Set \(\mathscr{F}^{\vee} = \mathscr{H}om_{\mathscr{O}_S}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{O}_S)\). We have canonical isomorphisms: \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}^{\vee}) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbf{O}_S) \simeq \mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}) \ , \] \[ \mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}^{\vee}) \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{V}(\mathscr{F}),\mathbf{O}_S) \simeq \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}). \]
Finally, we have the following proposition:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6.6

Let \(f : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{F}'\) be a morphism of locally free \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules of finite rank. For \(\mathbf{W}(f) : \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}) \to \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}')\) to be a monomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) identify \(\mathscr{F}\) locally with a direct factor of \(\mathscr{F}'\).

The direct assertion is essentially contained in (EGA 0.5.5.5). The converse assertion is practically obvious.
4.7. The category of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules.
Let G be an S-group affine over S and \(\mathscr{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module; then \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) is equipped with a structure of \(\mathbf{O}_S\)-module.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.7.1

A structure of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module on \(\mathscr{F}\) is a structure of \(h_G\text{-}\mathbf{O}_S\)-module on \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\). (cf. 3.2).

A morphism of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Modules is by definition a morphism of the associated \(h_G\text{-}\mathbf{O}_S\)-modules. We can therefore say that we have constructed the category \((G\text{-}\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.})\) just defined by the cartesian square: \[ \xymatrix{ (G\text{-}\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & (h_G\text{-}\mathbf{O}_S\text{-Mod.}) \ar[d] \\ \mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.} \ar[r]_-{\mathbf{W}} & \mathbf{O}_S\text{-Mod.} } \] The category \((G\text{-}\mathscr{O}_S\text{-Mod.})\) is abelian.

To give a structure of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module on \(\mathscr{F}\) is thus (by 3.2) to give a morphism of \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\)-groups \[ h_G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})). \] By virtue of 4.6.4, the datum of a morphism of S-functors \[ \rho : h_G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathbf{O}_S}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})) \] is equivalent to that of a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Modules \[ \mu : \mathscr{F} \longrightarrow \mathscr{A}(G) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{F}. \] To say that \(\rho\) is a morphism of \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\)-groups is then equivalent to saying that \(\mu\) satisfies the following axioms:

(CM 1) the following diagram is commutative \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{F} \ar[r]^-{\mu} \ar[d]_-{\mu} & \mathscr{F}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}(G) \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{Id}\otimes\Delta} \\ \mathscr{F}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}(G) \ar[r]_-{\mu\otimes\mathrm{Id}} & \mathscr{F}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}(G)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S}\mathscr{A}(G) } \]

(CM 2) the composite \[ \mathscr{F} \xrightarrow{\ \mu\ } \mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{A}(G) \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{Id}\otimes\varepsilon\ } \mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{O}_S \longrightarrow \mathscr{F} \] is the identity. These axioms (CM 1) and (CM 2) are those of the structure of comodule over the bigebra \(\mathscr{A}(G)\). In summary:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7.2

The preceding construction yields an equivalence of the category of quasi-coherent G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules (resp. G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules) with the category of quasi-coherent comodules (resp. comodules) over the \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-bigebra \(\mathscr{A}(G)\).

Suppose now that G is a diagonalizable group, that is, that \(\mathscr{A}(G)\) is the algebra of a commutative group M over the sheaf of rings \(\mathscr{O}_S\). If \(\mathscr{F}\) is an \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module, we have \[ \mathscr{F} \otimes \mathscr{A}(G) = \coprod_{m\in M} \mathscr{F} \otimes m\mathscr{O}_S. \] Giving a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules \[ \mu : \mathscr{F} \longrightarrow \mathscr{F} \otimes \mathscr{A}(G) \] is therefore equivalent to giving \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-endomorphisms \((\mu_m)_{m\in M}\) of \(\mathscr{F}\), such that for every section \(x\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) over an open subset of S, \((\mu_m(x))\) is a section of the direct sum \(\coprod_{m\in M} \mathscr{F}\) (this means that on every sufficiently small open subset, only finitely many of the restrictions of the \(\mu_m(x)\) are non-zero).

For \(\mu\) defined by \[ \mu(x) = \sum_{m\in M} \mu_m(x) \otimes m \] to satisfy (CM 1) (resp. (CM 2)), it is necessary and sufficient that we have \[ \mu_m \circ \mu_{m'} = \delta_{mm'}\mu_m, \qquad \Big(\text{resp.} \sum_{m\in M} \mu_m = \mathrm{Id}_{\mathscr{F}}\Big), \] which means that the \(\mu_m\) are pairwise orthogonal projectors with sum the identity. We have thus proved:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7.3

If \(G = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\) is a diagonalizable S-group, the category of quasi-coherent G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules (resp. of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules) is equivalent to the category of quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Modules (resp. of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules) graded of type M.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

If \(\mathscr{F}\) is equipped with a structure of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebra preserved by the operations of G, then the grading of G is an algebra grading. More precisely: The functor \(\mathscr{A} \mapsto \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}\) induces an equivalence between the category of quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-algebras graded of type M and the category opposite to that of S-schemes affine over S with S-group scheme of operators \(G = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7.4

Let G be a diagonalizable S-group. If \(0 \to \mathscr{F}_1 \to \mathscr{F}_2 \to \mathscr{F}_3 \to 0\) is an exact sequence of quasi-coherent G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules which splits as a sequence of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules, then it also splits as a sequence of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules.

Indeed, each of the \(\mathscr{F}_i\) is graded by \((\mathscr{F}_i)_m\), and for each \(m \in M\) the sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow (\mathscr{F}_1)_m \longrightarrow (\mathscr{F}_2)_m \longrightarrow (\mathscr{F}_3)_m \longrightarrow 0 \] of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules is split. The preceding proposition then implies the result.
5. Cohomology of groups
5.1. The standard complex.

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category, G a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group, O a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-ring and F a G-O-module. We set \[ \mathrm{C}^n(G,F) = \mathrm{Hom}(G^n, F) \ , \qquad \underline{\mathrm{C}}^n(G,F) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G^n, F), \qquad n \geqslant 0, \] where \(G^0\) is the final object \(\underline{e}\). Then \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^n(G,F)\) (resp. \(\mathrm{C}^n(G,F)\)) is equipped in an obvious way with a structure of O-module (resp. \(\Gamma(O)\)-module) and we have \[ \mathrm{C}^n(G,F) \cong \Gamma(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^n(G,F)) \ , \qquad \underline{\mathrm{C}}^n(G,F)(S) = \mathrm{C}^n(G_S, F_S). \] To give an element of \(\mathrm{C}^n(G,F)\) is to give, for each \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), an n-cochain of \(G(S)\) with values in \(F(S)\), functorially in S. The coboundary operator \[ \partial : \mathrm{C}^n(G(S), F(S)) \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}^{n+1}(G(S), F(S)), \] which, let us recall, is given by the formula \[ \partial f(g_1,\ldots,g_{n+1}) = g_1 f(g_2,\ldots,g_{n+1}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (-1)^i f(g_1,\ldots,g_i g_{i+1},\ldots,g_n) + (-1)^{n+1} f(g_1,\ldots,g_n), \] is functorial in S and therefore defines a homomorphism: \[ \partial : \mathrm{C}^n(G,F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}^{n+1}(G,F) \] such that \(\partial \circ \partial = 0\). We have thus defined a complex of abelian groups (and even of \(\Gamma(O)\)-modules), denoted \(\mathrm{C}^*(G,F)\). One defines in the same way the complex of O-modules \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^*(G,F)\), and we have: \[ \mathrm{C}^*(G,F) = \Gamma(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^*(G,F)). \] We denote by \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,F)\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^n(G,F)\)) the homology groups (resp. \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups) of the complex \(\mathrm{C}^*(G,F)\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^*(G,F)\)). We have in particular \[ \underline{\mathrm{H}}^0(G,F) = F^G \ , \qquad \mathrm{H}^0(G,F) = \Gamma(F^G). \] We now propose to show that the functors \(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^n\) (resp. \(\mathrm{H}^n\)) are indeed the derived functors of \(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^0\) (resp. \(\mathrm{H}^0\)). Let P be an O-module; consider the object \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,P)\) of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\). One can equip it with a structure of G-O-module by making G operate on the first factor and O on the second. Precisely, we have \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,P)(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_S(G_S, P_S)\), and we make \(g \in G(S)\) and \(a \in O(S)\) operate by the formulas: \[ (gf)(x) = f(xg) \ , \qquad (af)(x) = af(x) \ , \qquad x \in G(S'). \] Now let F be a G-O-module; denote by \(\mathrm{E}(F)\) the object \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,F)\) equipped with its preceding G-O-module structure. There is a morphism of G-O-modules \[ \mu_F : F \longrightarrow \mathrm{E}(F) \] defined in the following way: with every arrow \(S' \to S''\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), we must associate, functorially, a map from \(F(S')\) to the set \(\mathrm{Hom}(G(S'), F(S'))\); to do this, we associate with every \(f \in F(S')\) the map \(k_f\) defined by \(k_f(g) = gf\). One checks at once
that \(\mu_F\) is indeed compatible with the G-O-module structures and that it is even a monomorphism.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2.1

If G is representable, the functors \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,\ )\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^n(G,\ )\)) are the derived functors of the left exact functor \(\mathrm{H}^0(G,\ )\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^0(G,\ )\)) on the category of G-O-modules.

By virtue of well-known general results, it suffices to check that the \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,\ )\) (resp. \(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^n(G,\ )\)) form an effaceable cohomological functor in dimensions \(> 0\). Now \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^n(G,\ )\), considered as a functor on \((G\text{-}O\text{-Mod.})\) with values in the category of \((O\text{-Mod.})\), is exact. This shows that the \(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^n(G,\ )\) do form a cohomological functor. Since the functor \(\Gamma\) is exact, the same holds for the \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,\ )\). It will now suffice for us to prove:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.2

For every \(P \in \mathrm{Ob}(O\text{-Mod.})\), we have: \[ \mathrm{H}^n(G, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,P)) = 0, \qquad \underline{\mathrm{H}}^n(G, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,P)) = 0, \qquad n > 0. \]

It suffices for us to prove that \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}^*(G, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,P))\) and \(\mathrm{C}^*(G, \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,P))\) are homotopically trivial in dimensions \(> 0\). It even suffices to do so for the second, the corresponding result for the first being deduced from it by base change. Now we have the following homotopy operator: \[ (sf)(g_1,g_2,\ldots,g_n)(g) = f(g,g_0,g_1,\ldots,g_n)(e) \] where \(e\) denotes the identity element of \(G(S)\) and \(f\) an \((n+1)\)-cochain of \(G(S)\) with values in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,P)(S)\).
5.3. Cohomology of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules.

Let S be a scheme, G an S-group affine over S and \(\mathscr{F}\) a quasi-coherent G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module. One defines the cohomology groups of G with values in \(\mathscr{F}\) by \[ \mathrm{H}^n(G, \mathscr{F}) = \mathrm{H}^n(h_G, \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})) \] (for the notation, cf. 4.6).

In view of Proposition 4.6.4, this cohomology is computed in the following way: \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,\mathscr{F})\) is the n-th homology group of the complex \(\mathrm{C}^n(G,\mathscr{F})\) defined as follows: \[ \mathrm{C}^n(G,\mathscr{F}) = \Gamma(S, \underline{\mathrm{C}}^n(G,\mathscr{F})) \ , \qquad \underline{\mathrm{C}}^n(G,\mathscr{F}) = \mathscr{F} \otimes \underbrace{\mathscr{A}(G) \otimes \cdots \otimes \mathscr{A}(G)}_{n \text{ times}}. \] If \(f\) (resp. \(a_i\)) is a section of \(\mathscr{F}\) (resp. of \(\mathscr{A}(G)\)) over an open subset of S, we have \[ \partial(f\otimes a_1\otimes\cdots\otimes a_n) = \mu_{\mathscr{F}}(f)\otimes a_1\otimes a_2\otimes\cdots\otimes a_n \] \[ {} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (-1)^i f\otimes a_1\otimes\cdots\otimes \Delta a_i\otimes\cdots\otimes a_n + (-1)^{n+1} f\otimes a_1\otimes a_2\cdots\otimes a_n\otimes 1, \] where \(\mu_{\mathscr{F}} : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{A}(G)\) and \(\Delta : \mathscr{A}(G) \to \mathscr{A}(G)\otimes\mathscr{A}(G)\) describe the comodule structure of \(\mathscr{F}\). Let us note in passing that the cohomology of G with values in \(\mathscr{F}\)

therefore depends only on the comodule structure of \(\mathscr{F}\), and in particular only on the S-monoid structure of G. We have in particular \[ \mathrm{H}^0(G, \mathscr{F}) = \Gamma(S, \mathscr{F}^G), \] where \(\mathscr{F}^G\), the sheaf of invariants of \(\mathscr{F}\), is defined as the sheaf whose sections over the open subset U of S are the sections of \(\mathscr{F}\) over U whose inverse image in every \(S'\) over U is invariant under \(G(S')\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.1

Let S be an affine scheme and G an S-group affine and flat over S. The functors \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,\ )\) are the derived functors of \(\mathrm{H}^0(G,\ )\) on the category of quasi-coherent G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules.

Since S is affine, and since G is affine and flat, one sees at once that \(\mathrm{C}^*(G,\ )\) is indeed an exact functor on the preceding category. It only remains to construct, for every \(\mathscr{F}\), a monomorphism from \(\mathscr{F}\) into a G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module \(\mathrm{E}(\mathscr{F})\) such that \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,\mathrm{E}(\mathscr{F})) = 0\), \(n > 0\). Now consider \(\mathrm{E}(\mathscr{F}) = \mathscr{A}(G) \otimes \mathscr{F}\) equipped with the G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module structure defined by that of \(\mathscr{A}(G)\). Consider the canonical homomorphism \(\mu_{\mathscr{F}} : \mathscr{F} \to \mathrm{E}(\mathscr{F})\); axiom (CM 1) of 4.7.2 says exactly that \(\mu_{\mathscr{F}}\) is a morphism of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules. It now suffices to note that the image of \(\mu_{\mathscr{F}}\) under the functor \(\mathbf{W}\) is none other than the effacement \(\mu_{\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})} : \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}) \to \mathrm{E}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}))\) defined in 5.2 (compare with Corollary 1 to Proposition 4.6.4). Note now that axiom (CM 2) shows that, considered as an \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module, \(\mathscr{F}\) is a direct factor of \(\mathrm{E}(\mathscr{F})\). This implies:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.3.2

Let S be an affine scheme and G an S-group affine and flat; suppose that every exact sequence \(0 \to \mathscr{F}_1 \to \mathscr{F}_2 \to \mathscr{F}_3 \to 0\) of quasi-coherent G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules which splits as a sequence of \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules also splits as a sequence of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-modules. Then the functors \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,\ )\), \(n > 0\), are zero (or, what amounts to the same thing, the functor \(\mathrm{H}^0(G,\ )\) is exact).

Indeed, by the hypothesis, the sequence \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathrm{E}(\mathscr{F}) \to \mathrm{E}(\mathscr{F})/\mathscr{F} \to 0\) is split; \(\mathscr{F}\) is therefore a direct factor of \(\mathrm{E}(\mathscr{F})\), and the cohomology of the latter is zero.
From this and Proposition 4.7.4 one immediately deduces:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.3

Let S be an affine scheme and G a diagonalizable S-group. For every quasi-coherent G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module \(\mathscr{F}\), we have \(\mathrm{H}^n(G,\mathscr{F}) = 0\), \(n > 0\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

Proposition 5.3.2 remains valid when G is not necessarily flat over S; the proof then appeals to relative cohomology.
